title: Bloodborne: Speedrun: The Board Game, or Time's Up! Stop Having Fun!
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=KNT1a6LakZI

Daniel (@dungeondive) is deeply frustrated by Bloodborne: The Board Game from CMON, because it does so much right and one thing wrong enough to sink it for him: the timer. A strip of cardboard that ticks up every round and every trip to the Hunter's Dream, ending the campaign cold if it fills — which forces you to speed-run a game whose pleasures all lie in slowing down. He's not anti-timer (he praises Champions of Hara and Legends of Andor), but this one actively pushes you away from the best parts.

And those parts are excellent: the trick-weapon combat that transforms mid-fight just like the video game, speed-based attacks you must commit to, a robust card-driven campaign with analog "memory" and branching side quests, and a satisfying Hunter's Dream deck-building loop. The trouble is every one of those systems costs time, so the timer punishes engagement — avoid combat, skip leveling, rush the trigger. He argues it's a band-aid over a self-inflicted wound: a video game with no "game over" forced into needing one. Great for efficiency-puzzle lovers; for Daniel, straight to the sale pile.

----

Daniel says Bloodborne's timer turns a dungeon crawl into a dungeon sprint, punishing the very systems he wants to enjoy. Do timers add welcome tension for you, or do they wreck the experience?
